title: Designing Reliable Agentic Systems in 2026
author: For developers building real systems · April 18, 2026

slides.title: Designing Reliable Agentic Systems in 2026
slides.subtitle: From Experimental Agents to Production-Grade Architectures
slides.body: Distributed systems thinking applied to autonomous AI · Not a beginner's guide

slides.title: Why Reliability Became the Hard Problem
bullets: Agents succeed in demos; they fail under real-world load and ambiguity
Core failure modes: non-determinism, tool failures, cascading cost spikes
2026 shift: the industry moved focus from capability to stability
Broken workflows cost more — in trust, time, and money — than bad UX
Key question: can your agent recover from its own mistakes?

slides.title: Single-Agent Logic → Distributed Execution
left: ReAct-style: plan → act → observe → repeat
One context window, one thread of execution
Sequential tool calls with no parallelism
One failure = full restart from scratch
Orchestration = a system prompt
right: Graph-based execution with parallel agent branches
Agents as composable, independently deployable services
Shared state, async messaging, partial failure recovery
Orchestration layer is the actual product being built
Coordination > raw model intelligence

slides.title: State Management: Event-Sourced Agents
slides.code:
class AgentState:
    def checkpoint(self, step: int, ctx: dict) -> str:
        event = StateEvent(step=step, context=ctx, ts=now())
        self.store.append(event)  # immutable log
        return event.id

    def resume(self, checkpoint_id: str) -> AgentContext:
        events = self.store.up_to(checkpoint_id)
        return AgentContext.replay(events)  # deterministic replay

# Stateless agents cannot resume. Checkpoints enable:
# - fault tolerance across tool failures
# - cost-efficient retries (skip completed steps)
# - full auditability of agent decisions

slides.title: Orchestration Patterns in 2026
left: Supervisor–Worker: one orchestrator, many executors
Hierarchical: planner → sub-planners → executors
Deterministic flow with embedded LLM decision points
Easier to audit, trace, and roll back on failure
Best fit for sequential, high-stakes workflows
right: Peer-to-peer agents sharing distributed task queues
Async execution + exponential-backoff retry loops
Event-driven: agents react to shared state changes
Higher throughput for embarrassingly parallel tasks
Harder to reason about globally — tracing is mandatory

slides.title: Failure Handling & Recovery Strategies
bullets: Retry loops alone are insufficient — add circuit breakers
Fallback tools + guardrails handle out-of-distribution inputs
Human-in-the-loop for irreversible or high-blast-radius actions
Treat timeouts and partial completions as first-class outcomes
Core principle: design for failure modes, not just the happy path

slides.title: Observability for Agent Systems
left: Structured decision traces, not raw log lines
Tool call sequences and their downstream side effects
State transitions logged at every agent boundary
Semantic diffs between runs for regression detection
Replay + time-travel debugging for incident analysis
right: Latency: per-task and per-subtask breakdown
Cost per successful task completion
Tool call failure rate segmented by tool type
Context window utilization over the task lifetime
Human escalation rate as a leading quality signal

slides.title: Designing for Real Use Cases
left: Autonomous CI/CD pipeline agents
Incident debugging + root-cause analysis
Data enrichment and classification pipelines
Code review and security audit automation
Multi-step procurement and approval flows
right: Constrained scope → testable, predictable behavior
Easier to validate correctness end-to-end
Faster path from prototype to production deployment
Lower blast radius and rollback cost on failure
Stakeholder trust is built incrementally, not assumed

slides.title: The Near Future (2026–2028)
bullets: Agent-to-agent protocols (A2A, MCP) becoming interoperability standards
Hybrid systems: LLM reasoning embedded in deterministic rule engines
Edge-deployed agents for latency-sensitive and air-gapped environments
Security, identity, and audit trails for agents are now table stakes
Engineering discipline will matter more than prompt engineering
